great. let me know how it goes. start from the GToolkit/Glamorous Tour
Interesting. Are there other ways than documentation that foster collaboration? Something less tiresome to maintain.

root.text:
Yeah, I should elaborate. Well-written documentation can be _great_ for collaboration. But well-written documentation can be _really_ hard to do, _even_ when the incentives are perfectly aligned (very rare even in open source; the world is complex). So lately I try to at least talk about the thing right in front of the thing rather than on some other page all by itself. That way at least if I screw up how I describe the thing, at least the reader has a chance at trying to cross-triangulate what I mean by looking at and running the thing itself.

But you're a Smalltalk guy. I suspect I'm preaching to the choir :slightly_smiling_face:

text: Documentation does not have to be tiresome to maintain :slightly_smiling_face:

What I find interesting is telling a story about and with the system I am building. To do that I am forced to build the story in the system. This often leads to visualizations that describe internal aspects, and this leads to live documentation.
That sounds like a fun dissertation! Would like to see it in the future.
I think writing documentation would be really fun if we had the right tools to do good storytelling. That's why I'm excited about efforts toward literate programming.
I think literate programming is interesting, too. But, we should not limit storytelling to words. Live and visual interactions are too powerful to leave out :slightly_smiling_face:

It seems obvious that the person who knows most about the system needs to document it. However, it turns out _good_ documentation often comes from other people, who had to understand the system from scratch long after it has been designed.

It’s hard to look at our own creations with a Beginner’s Mind again, but that’s really helpful, if not essential, to write good documentation.

And I’m not sure if writing documentation itself can be more fun — I’d like to think so. For sure though, there are people who like writing documentation. So it’s great if you have a chance to collaborate with those.
Precisely
I do think that writing documentation can be fun if you have the right development experience for it

Since about 15 years I am practicing demo-driven development. This means that I drive my development form the point of view of the demo that I will give with and about it. This forces me to build the story into the system, including the visuals.
And here I am referring to any details about my system. I think everything about my system should be explainable to a newcomer in minutes

It seems like large amount of documentation could be generated if we had proper programming environments.
Version incompatabilities, examples of usage, changes in API -- all of this can be generated.

What else is left? Stories, grand narratives? Use cases?
Explaining what a function/method/class does :stuck_out_tongue:
explaining _why_ a design or implementation choice was made
Thanks for the link to your comment. Very interesting :+1::skin-tone-3:

There's the question of content (what should be written, what aspects covered). But there's also the problem of the representation medium - most documentation today is off to the side, completely disconnected from the artifacts it is talking about. This is a fundamentally fractured model. You not only have out of date stuff, but constantly jump back and forth. Instead of a clear distinction between 'docs' and 'code', ideally there would be a hypermedium with various views at different levels of detail: high level views (system design, narratives) all the way to low level views (code) - all of them interconnected and traversible.

That's an interesting discussion! I'd be neat if more people would collaborate on documentation, and as <@U5STGTB3J> pointed out, novices to a project are especially important as collaborators.
I wonder if more people would edit documentation if it wasn't owned by the project's owner. Think about having wikipedia-like editing right within your documentation. Especially if I'm new to a project, I'm always afraid to screw up other people's documentation by writing something stupid. I wonder if knowing that the next regular guy who reads my changes could easily improve or correct them would lower the bar to start collaborating.
Another option could be to include comments into each documentation page. People are used to providing feedback if they can, so they would probably point out a lot of issues that could then be used by maintainers to improve documentation.
Using current tools (IRC, github issues / PRs) all of this is possible already, but I think that making it easier and integrating it better into the documentation itself could make a huge difference.
GToolkit is doing going in this direction, it seems. E.g. you can have markdown like docs within which you embed live results of relevant queries on the code repository.

I agree that code and documentation should be as close together as possible. When talking about different views, *projectional editors* come to mind. The data structure they use internally is more like an AST and therefore more expressive than the "source text" commonly used today. You could add a "comment" attribute to each node type (module, class, even parts of an expression) or introduce additional node types only suited for documentation (tables, graphs, ...).

In general, PL designers seem to not think about program comprehensibility beyond code syntax and allowing you to insert a couple of characters here and there as part of a comment. I wonder how a programming system that focuses on being understandable by humans would look like. Rich documentation, visible connections / dependencies between components, abstract overview projections, ...
The 'syntax' is important only because the def facto medium is directly edited plain text files. To get all the good things you have to give that up, which unfortunately leaves you in a bubble.
yeah, moving away from text you lose a ton of nice properties.  I’m thinking searchability, easy generation / transformation, copy/paste.
But I’m sure alternative media have still _other_ properties that I just don’t know/appreciate because I’m so oriented to text-based programs
All of those things get better, no? It's just your text oriented operating systems and tooling don't work for you anymore.
right, thats 40+ years of accumulated tooling
Yes.. and do we want the next 40 years to be different, or the same? :wink:

haha, for sure. I don’t know specifically what you’re thinking of, I’ve just read other kinda of proposals for new program _authoring_ environments that don’t take into account program _comprehension_. Which I get concerned about.

Hi all! I'm a Twitter fan of <@U5TCAFTD3> and the work of lots of people here.

I've dabbled with programming environment projects like <http://dalsegno.ballingt.com/> mostly because I was trying to copy some work <https://maryrosecook.com> was doing, finally signed up for this Slack while watching Chris Granger's LIVE talk. I'm excited about programming education (I volunteer with Software Carpentry and worked at the Recurse Center for 4 years) and have reached the point where I've been excited about possible programming futures for long enough to start being a little questioning, but I'm not cynical yet :slightly_smiling_face:

Oh I'm interesting and occasionally involved in the IPython/Jupyter world, and I still like REPLs and wrote a new terminal frontend for bpython - I liked the rewind feature and made some changes to it that makes it more exciting for live-coding.

attachments.title: Mary Rose Cook
attachments.text: Essays, talks and projects by Mary Rose Cook

text: Currently in SF working at Dropbox, I spend some time in NYC too.
TBH, I'm not bringing solutions, only problems. But one creeping realization I have is that fixing the problem might mean first changing some of the underlying assumptions and entrenched realities.

'Comprehension' is key terminology here. Most people focus on 'readability' in the small rather than the sorts of global activities programmers need to do that look a lot more like reading comprehension.

I tend to think text is a perfectly fine way to do comprehension. We just need to do a better job (in some fairly straight-forward, actionable ways) within the context of existing text-based systems. Maybe going beyond text will also be helpful, but that seems an open question. For now, text is an assumption I'm content to retain. <@U8A5MS6R1> and I have argued this before :slightly_smiling_face:
pulling out that distinction between readability and comprehension is useful

We also can embed various views of examples. Examples have assertions, like tests, but they also return the object. Combined with the fact that objects can have different facets and with the fact that we can embed these artifacts in a document, it becomes a highly effective mechanism. We have a simple example framework that replaces the testing framework and we now practice example-driven development.

Great point. I'm careful now to use the phrase 'plain text files'.  Things like spreadsheets, Subtext and <http://infra-structure.org|infra-structure.org> are excluded by text oriented systems like Git. You still have text in these systems, mainly at the boundary with the reader (not necessarily as a large blob though, but organized within a different structure). If you have projectional interfaces, much of the content could be text. But why version and store large blobs of text when you really want history and different projections of the richer interconnected structures that are being represented? That's my thinking anyway.
and me! :slightly_smiling_face: It was great to meet  you all--or see you again after some time!
and me! :slightly_smiling_face: It was great to meet  you all--or see you again after some time!
Ah, that's a good distinction that you've had to remind me of a few times now. Very subtle and seems to easily fall off my head.
